edges.description: Abnormal developing brain network function contributes to early seizures.
Genetic etiologies and epileptic activity contribute to impaired development.

nodes.description: Fenfluramine is being piloted as adjunctive treatment for selected developmental and epileptic encephalopathy subtypes, including STXBP1 encephalopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Fenfluramine for the Treatment of Different Types of Developmental and Epileptic Encephalopathies: a Pilot Trial Exploring Epileptic and Non-epileptic Outcomes
evidence.snippet: The main goal is to assess changes in seizure frequency comparing before and after treatment with fenfluramine in five specific types of developmental and epileptic encephalopathies (DEEs).

nodes.description: Anticonvulsant therapy is used to reduce seizure frequency and severity, although many EOEE subtypes remain pharmaco-resistant.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiologie Basierte Therapie Von früh Beginnenden Epileptischen Enzephalopathien
evidence.snippet: Genetic epileptic encephalopathies (EEs) are a group of very rare and severe, pharmaco-resistant epilepsy forms characterized by an early onset, e.g. first years of life, and an often severe developmental delay.

nodes.description: The same underlying etiologies that generate epileptic encephalopathy can also impair psychomotor, cognitive, speech, and motor development.

evidence.reference_title: Whole Exome Sequencing as a First-Line Molecular Genetic Test in Developmental and Epileptic Encephalopathies
evidence.snippet: Developmental and epileptic encephalopathies (DEE) are severe neurodevelopmental disorders characterized by recurrent, usually early-onset, epileptic seizures accompanied by developmental impairment often related to both underlying genetic etiology and abnormal epileptiform activity.

nodes.description: Heterogeneous genetic etiologies disrupt neuronal development and synaptic signaling in the developing brain, producing early epileptic network hyperexcitability.

evidence.reference_title: Whole Exome Sequencing as a First-Line Molecular Genetic Test in Developmental and Epileptic Encephalopathies
evidence.snippet: Developmental and epileptic encephalopathies (DEE) are severe neurodevelopmental disorders characterized by recurrent, usually early-onset, epileptic seizures accompanied by developmental impairment often related to both underlying genetic etiology and abnormal epileptiform activity.

nodes.description: Seizures begin in the neonatal or infantile period and may vary by type and severity across the syndrome and its subtypes.

evidence.reference_title: Whole Exome Sequencing as a First-Line Molecular Genetic Test in Developmental and Epileptic Encephalopathies
evidence.snippet: Developmental and epileptic encephalopathies (DEE) are severe neurodevelopmental disorders characterized by recurrent, usually early-onset, epileptic seizures accompanied by developmental impairment often related to both underlying genetic etiology and abnormal epileptiform activity.

nodes.description: Treatment selection can be tailored to the defective ion channel or genetic subtype where mechanism suggests a drug response.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiologie Basierte Therapie Von früh Beginnenden Epileptischen Enzephalopathien
evidence.snippet: The goal of this monocentric non-blinded non-placebo controlled phase IIb study is the evaluation of the effectivity of anticonvulsive drugs specifically working on the ion channels defective in some subtypes of EEs in order to establish a standard and individualized therapy for these rare diseases based on the specific genetic defect.
